explanation_md: `arr[0]` is 10 and `arr[3]` is 40. Their sum is 50, which is stored in `arr[1]` and then printed. The original value 20 in `arr[1]` is overwritten.

prompt_md: A program allocates a dynamic array with `int* p = new int[8];`. Which statement correctly frees this memory?

options.d: No call is needed — heap memory is freed automatically when `p` goes out of scope

explanation_md: `new[]` must always be paired with `delete[]`. Using plain `delete` is undefined behavior for arrays. `free()` is the C function and must not be mixed with `new`. Heap memory is never freed automatically for raw pointers — that is exactly the problem smart pointers and `std::vector` solve.
After the loop, `arr[0]` is 1 and `arr[4]` is 5. Their sum is 6. The `{}` initialiser zeroes all elements first, but the loop overwrites them with 1 through 5.

options.b: The loop condition `i <= n` accesses `arr[n]`, which is one past the end — out-of-bounds access
options.c: A dynamic array cannot be initialised with a brace list
options.d: The variable `n` must be `const` to use it as an array size with `new[]`

explanation_md: Valid indices for an array of size `n` are 0 through `n-1`. The condition `i <= n` causes the loop to execute when `i == n`, which reads `arr[n]` — one element past the end of the allocated block. This is undefined behavior. The fix is `i < n`.

prompt_md: What happens to the elements of `int* p = new int[6]{};` immediately after allocation?

options.a: All six elements contain unpredictable garbage values
options.b: All six elements are zero-initialised to 0
options.c: The first element is 0 and the rest are undefined
options.d: The array is not allocated; the `{}` causes a compile error

explanation_md: An empty brace initialiser `{}` on a `new[]` expression value-initialises all elements. For `int`, value-initialisation means zero-initialisation — every element is set to 0. Without `{}`, elements would be uninitialised (contain garbage).
